turns.content: I tried to look at the `zip` method on iterators but I keep thinking it just chains them together like a flat list. In my last code review, someone used it with two vectors but I couldn't tell how they were getting pairs back out without writing extra loops or manual indexing.

That's actually a common misunderstanding when you come back to Rust after so many years. `zip` doesn't flatten the data; it creates an iterator that yields `(T1, T2)` tuples for every pair of elements from the two input iterators. If you're looking at a collection of pairs like a flat vector of structs, what you really need is `unzip`, which takes a single iterator over pairs and separates them back into two independent iterators so you can iterate each one separately without carrying around the paired data.

Right, so I was treating it like a simple concatenation but actually it's doing element-wise pairing. But then how do I get that flat vector of structs back into separate vectors? I keep thinking `zip` does both directions automatically.

You don't need two different methods for the same operation; you just use the right one depending on what you want to achieve. If you have a single iterator yielding pairs and want to split them, that's `unzip`. But if you already have two separate iterators and want to combine them into pairs, that's `zip`. The key is recognizing which direction your data flow needs to go: are you combining two sources or splitting one source? Once you see it as a directional tool rather than a bidirectional switch, the distinction becomes much clearer.
